one_line_takeaway: SRAGU reweights AGU's per-parameter unlearning sensitivities by a layer-wise heavy-tailed spectral-stability gate, reportedly lowering behavioral divergence-to-gold and membership leakage versus AGU -- but the retrieved PDF stops at Table 7, omitting the mechanistic and stress-test evidence its own roadmap promises, and contains at least one clearly anomalous accuracy entry.
key_finding: On the retrieved tables, SRAGU consistently improves gold-alignment (lower epsilon_pred and D_KL) over AGU and the other baselines while keeping retained accuracy competitive with or slightly above AGU. E.g., CIFAR-10 adversarial deletion at 10% (Table 4): SRAGU epsilon_pred 0.224 / D_KL 0.167 vs AGU 0.356 / 0.278, at similar accuracy (62.98 vs 62.61; ORTR 74.25). Under 30% influence-style deletion, SRAGU also drives MIA-AUC back toward the ORTR floor: CIFAR-10 (Table 6) MIA-AUC 0.530 vs AGU 0.667 (ORTR 0.511) with epsilon_pred 0.030 vs 0.162; CIFAR-100 (Table 7) MIA-AUC 0.646 vs AGU 0.795 with accuracy 56.37 vs 45.51. The paper reads these as evidence that concentrating unlearning in spectrally stable layers reduces both behavioral residual influence and empirical membership leakage.
core_contribution: The paper proposes SRAGU (Statistical-Roughness Adaptive Gradient Unlearning), a drop-in extension of the prior AGU (Adaptive Gradient Unlearning) baseline for approximate machine unlearning. AGU weights each parameter's unlearning update by a per-parameter sensitivity score R_j (mean squared gradient magnitude over the forget set). SRAGU's own novel contribution is to modulate those sensitivities by a LAYER-WISE stability signal: for each layer it forms the Gram matrix C_l = (1/m) W_l^T W_l, fits a power law lambda^(-xi_l) to the top tau-fraction of its eigenvalues to obtain a heavy-tailed (WeightWatcher-style) exponent xi_l, maps xi_l to a bounded 'in-range gate' weight nu_l in (0,1) that is largest when 2<xi_l<4, and reweights sensitivities as R'_j = R_j * nu_l(j). The intended effect is to concentrate unlearning motion in spectrally stable layers and damp updates in brittle (undertrained, xi<=2) or overfit (rigid, xi>=4) layers. The update form and stopping rule are otherwise identical to AGU. NOTE: heavy-tailed self-regularization, the AGU baseline, SISA/SCRUB/SalUn/Boundary, and MIA auditing are all CITED prior work; SRAGU's own delta is specifically the spectral-stability reweighting of AGU sensitivities.
threat_model: The security frame is the 'right to erasure' / data deletion setting: a data owner requests that specific training records (the forget set D_forget) no longer influence a deployed model, and retraining from scratch (ORTR) is too expensive. Two adversary notions appear, and the paper is careful to distinguish them. (1) 'Adversarial deletion' means WORST-CASE forget-set CONSTRUCTION: an adversary ranks training points using ONLY the pre-unlearning model phi_0 (by high loss, low classification margin, high gradient norm, or influence-style gradient alignment) and picks the top-r fraction as the forget set. This is explicitly NOT an adaptive attacker interacting with the unlearning procedure -- rankings are computed once at phi_0 with no post-unlearning peeking. (2) A membership-inference attacker (threshold-based on predicted probabilities) is used as a complementary privacy AUDIT, treating forget-set points as should-be-forgotten members; MIA-AUC near 0.50 is the goal. The paper does not claim any formal (e.g., differential-privacy or certified-removal) guarantee -- leakage is measured empirically via behavioral alignment to the gold retrained model.
limitations: GROUNDING/COMPLETENESS: The paper's own experimental roadmap (Table 1) advertises Tables 8-22 and Sections 5.5-5.16 (efficiency/memory, deletion-ratio sweep, architecture generalization, layer-wise mechanistic profiling of nu_l/Q_l/A_l, unlearning trajectories, hyperparameter-sensitivity stress, a full MIA audit, limitations, and future work), but the retrieved 6-page PDF ENDS at Table 7 (CIFAR-100 cross-dataset). So the central mechanistic evidence the paper promises (that update allocation A_l actually correlates with nu_l/xi_l) is NOT present in the available text -- the claimed layer-wise causal story is asserted, not shown here. INTERNAL CONSISTENCY: Table 4 CIFAR-100 'adversarial' SRAGU accuracy is 71.34, wildly out of line with its own random (51.69) and class-specific (51.49) columns and implausibly near the ORTR adversarial value (71.62), suggesting a transcription/fabrication error that undermines confidence in the table. The text also switches naming ('IMU methods') without definition. METHODOLOGICAL: 'adversarial' is only static worst-case forget-set selection, never an adaptive attacker; MIA is a single threshold-based auditor (no LiRA/shadow-model attack); no formal removal guarantee; Section 4's bounds are informal and assumption-dependent; spectral fits are acknowledged to be noisy for small layers.

quantitative_results: CIFAR-10 adversarial, 10% (Table 4): SRAGU epsilon_pred 0.224 / D_KL 0.167 / Acc 62.98 vs AGU 0.356 / 0.278 / 62.61; ORTR Acc 74.25
MNIST adversarial, 10% (Table 4): SRAGU epsilon_pred 0.052 vs AGU 0.104; SRAGU Acc 88.93 vs ORTR 96.10
CIFAR-10 High-loss adversary, 10% (Table 3): SRAGU epsilon_pred 0.056 / D_KL 0.075 vs AGU 0.162 / 0.278
CIFAR-10, 30% influence-style deletion (Table 6): SRAGU MIA-AUC 0.530 / epsilon_pred 0.030 / Acc 61.89 vs AGU 0.667 / 0.162 / 61.22; ORTR MIA-AUC 0.511
MNIST, 30% influence-style deletion (Table 5): SRAGU MIA-AUC 0.514 / Acc 89.33 vs AGU 0.598 / 87.98; ORTR MIA-AUC 0.507
CIFAR-100, 30% influence-style deletion (Table 7): SRAGU MIA-AUC 0.646 / epsilon_pred 0.051 / Acc 56.37 vs AGU 0.795 / 0.271 / 45.51; ORTR MIA-AUC 0.521
Anomaly: Table 4 CIFAR-100 adversarial SRAGU Acc reported as 71.34 vs its own random 51.69 / class-specific 51.49 (likely erroneous)
baselines_compared: AGU (post-hoc, influence/sensitivity-style; the direct predecessor SRAGU extends)
